one_line_summary: Laboratory experiments on induced tension fractures in granite, basalt, and marble demonstrate that the cubic law Q/Δh = (C/f)(2b)^3 holds for apertures 4–250 μm under normal stresses up to 20 MPa, whether fractures are open or closed; deviations from ideal parallel plates are captured by a factor f ranging from 1.04 to 1.65.
research_question: Is the cubic law valid for laminar fluid flow in a deformable rock fracture when the surfaces are in contact and aperture decreases under normal stress?
study_area_data: Homogeneous intact samples of granite, basalt, and marble from quarries in California; matrix permeabilities negligible.
Artificial tension fractures created by modified Brazilian loading method, yielding surfaces orthogonal to cylindrical axis for radial flow and a horizontal tension fracture for straight flow.
methods: Radial flow tests on cylindrical samples (0.15 m diameter, length 0.17–0.21 m) with a central 0.022 m diameter hole; outward radial flow.
Straight flow test on a rectangular granite block (0.121 m × 0.207 m × 0.155 m) with a sealed central groove for water distribution; flow along the fracture length.
Axial loading up to 20 MPa using a Riehle testing machine; piston also used to hold fractures open for initial open conditions.

Fracture deformation measured by three linear variable differential transformers (LVDTs) spaced 120° apart (radial) or three LVDTs across the block (straight), capable of detecting changes as small as 0.4 μm.
Hydraulic head difference Δh of 20 m of water (runs 1,2) and 0.5 m (run 3) to keep flow laminar.
Apparent aperture 2b_a determined from maximum fracture deformation ΔV_m and net deformation ΔV: 2b_a = ΔV_m − ΔV; true aperture 2b = 2b_a + 2b_r with residual aperture 2b_r treated as an unknown.

Least-squares minimization of Σ ω_i [log(Q/Δh)_i − log(C) − n log(2b_a + 2b_r)_i]^2 to fit exponent n and 2b_r, then fixing n=3 to fit factor f and 2b_r via Σ ω_i [log(Q/Δh)_i − log(C/f) − 3 log(2b_a + 2b_r)_i]^2, using weighting factor ω_i = 0.5 |log(Q/Δh)_{i+1} − log(Q/Δh)_{i-1}|.
key_findings: The cubic law Q/Δh = (C/f)(2b)^3 is valid for laminar flow in deformable rock fractures whether surfaces are open or in contact under normal stress, for apertures from 4 μm to 250 μm and stresses up to 20 MPa.
The exponent n obtained from least-squares fits is within 3% of 3 (values 3.01–3.10) for all rock types and flow geometries.
The deviation factor f accounting for roughness and non-parallelism ranges from 1.04 to 1.65; f generally decreases with repeated loading cycles as fracture surfaces become better mated.
Permeability is uniquely defined by fracture aperture and is independent of the loading/unloading stress history; hysteresis and permanent set do not alter the cubic law relationship.

The process of fracture closing under normal stress is controlled by the strength of asperities in contact, which maintain flow pathways; the strong (2b)^3 dependence means small aperture changes dominate flow behavior, masking other geometrical changes.
Results are independent of rock type (granite, basalt, marble).

evidence.evidence: For granite with straight flow, run 1, least-squares fit gave n=3.04 and residual aperture 2b_r=9.0 μm; calculated 2b_r from cubic law directly was 7.9 μm.
evidence.conditions: Straight flow, granite, run 1, loading and unloading, Δh=20 m.
evidence.notes: n close to 3; residual aperture from fit and direct calculation are consistent.

evidence.evidence: For granite with radial flow, run 2, fitted f=1.29, 2b_r=3.8 μm; cubic-law calculation gave 3.2 μm.
evidence.notes: f > 1, indicating deviation from ideal parallel plates.

evidence.evidence: For marble with radial flow, run 3, f=1.05 and 2b_r fitted=18.2 μm, calculated from cubic law also 18.1 μm; initial aperture was 123.0 μm, and surfaces were open during unloading.
evidence.conditions: Radial flow, marble, run 3, Δh=0.5 m, fracture opened during unloading cycle.
evidence.notes: Shows validity of cubic law from open to closed condition; f close to 1.

evidence.evidence: The friction factor diagram (f vs. Re) shows data for granite straight flow follow f=1.21 (run 1–2) and f=1.04 (run 3); for radial flow granite, f up to 1.49.
evidence.conditions: Laminar flow; Reynolds numbers based on inner radius for radial cases.
evidence.notes: Graphical confirmation of cubic law with f-factor correction; radial flow shows larger f due to more complex roughness effects.

evidence.evidence: Basalt radial flow run 2 gave f=1.65, the largest deviation; a rock chip broke off around the center borehole, potentially altering the flow field.
evidence.conditions: Radial flow, basalt, run 2, modified inner radius assumption from 0.011 m to 0.02 m.
evidence.notes: Largest f factor observed; experimental artefact may have influenced result.

limitations: Only normal stresses were applied; shear deformation and its effect on flow were not studied.
Experiments conducted at room temperature; thermal expansion effects on aperture not investigated.
Potential size effect not addressed in these tests (mentioned as open issue).
Basalt sample experienced a rock chip around the borehole, possibly affecting flow results.
Marble runs 1 and 2 showed marked deviations from the cubic law at apertures below 10 μm due to experimental difficulties.
The cubic law was tested up to a minimum aperture of 4 μm under a maximum normal stress of 20 MPa; higher stresses or smaller apertures not explored.
assumptions_conditions: Laminar flow of a viscous incompressible fluid (water) under isothermal conditions.
Darcy's law is valid; Q ∝ Δh for a given aperture.
Fracture surfaces are idealized as parallel plates, with deviations accounted for by factor f.
Matrix permeability is negligible compared to fracture flow.
No chemical reactions or mineral dissolution/precipitation.
Deformation of fracture is purely normal; no shear displacement.
The true aperture is the sum of an apparent (measured) and a constant residual aperture for each run.
The contact areas deform elastically without significant change in contact area under the applied stress levels.
variables_parameters: C (proportional constant, L²T⁻¹; C = (ρg/12μ) × (W/L) for straight flow, C = (2πρg/12μ ln(rₑ/r_w)) for radial flow)
ω (weighting factor for least squares, dimensionless)
ΔV (net fracture deformation, L), ΔV_m (maximum deformation, L), ΔV_t (total deformation, L), ΔV_r (rock deformation, L)

reusable_claims.claim: The cubic law Q/Δh = (C/f)(2b)³ is valid for laminar flow in rock fractures with apertures from 4 to 250 μm and normal stresses up to 20 MPa, independent of rock type (granite, basalt, marble) and stress history.
reusable_claims.condition: Laminar flow, negligible matrix permeability, no shear movement, ambient temperature, water as fluid.
reusable_claims.limitation: Not validated for shear deformation, thermal effects, or apertures < 4 μm.

reusable_claims.claim: Deviations from the ideal parallel-plate cubic law due to fracture roughness and non-parallelism can be captured by a factor f, with values between 1.04 and 1.65 for tension fractures in crystalline rocks.
reusable_claims.condition: Induced tension fractures, cyclic loading, radial and straight flow.
reusable_claims.limitation: f may depend on degree of mating, loading cycles, and flow geometry; larger f for radial than straight flow in granite.

reusable_claims.claim: Permeability of a fracture under normal load is uniquely determined by the aperture and is independent of loading/unloading paths.
reusable_claims.limitation: Not tested with shear or chemical alteration.

reusable_claims.claim: The transition from laminar to turbulent flow in fractures occurs at a critical Reynolds number of about 2300–2400.
reusable_claims.condition: Open or closed fractures with parallel-plate analogy.
reusable_claims.limitation: Data from prior work; not directly tested in the present experiments.

candidate_methods: [[least-squares fitting of power-law exponent n and residual aperture]]
[[radial flow test with LVDT displacement monitoring]]
[[friction factor vs. Reynolds number correlation for fractures]]
[[derivation of fracture aperture from deformation measurements]]
connections: Builds on earlier cubic law derivations by Boussinesq (1868), Lomize (1951), Romm (1966), and Louis (1969) for open fractures.

Contrasts with Sharp (1970) and Sharp & Maini (1972) who proposed an exponent of 2 for fracture flow; the present work reinterprets their data as consistent with cubic law when true aperture is used (as noted by Gale, 1975).
Relates to Lomize's (1951) roughness factor ε and empirical correction ψ = 96/Re [1+ (ε/2b)] which is analogous to the factor f introduced here.
Connects to the concept of hydraulic conductivity of a fracture K_f = (2b)²ρg/12μ and Darcy's law, extending it to closed fractures under stress.
open_questions: How does the cubic law behave when shear deformation is superimposed on normal stress?
What is the effect of elevated temperature on fracture aperture and flow due to thermal expansion?
Is there a size effect when scaling laboratory results to field-scale fractures?
What are the detailed mechanics of asperity deformation and contact area change at higher stresses or under chemical weathering?
Can a universal f factor be predicted from fracture surface roughness statistics?
coverage_notes: The provided fragments cover the abstract, introduction, laboratory procedures, results (including tables and figures), discussion, conclusions, and notation, giving complete coverage of the paper's main content.
